prompt:
We template our service config files with a pile of sed in the deploy script and I'm done with it — last week it substituted inside a commented-out block and half-broke checkout. I want a real renderer: package envrender, root of module go-envrender, stdlib only.

Two entry points:
- Expand(src string, env map[string]string, opts Options) (string, error) — pure string substitution. The environment is ALWAYS the injected map; never touch os.Environ, I need renders reproducible in CI.
- RenderFile(path string, env map[string]string, opts Options) (string, error) — same substitution plus include handling (below).

Substitution grammar:
- ${VAR} — value of VAR. Names are [A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*.
- ${VAR:-default} — default when VAR is unset or empty; ${VAR-default} — default only when unset. Default text is literal: runs to the first }, no substitution inside it, may be empty.
- $$ — one literal $ (that's the escape; $${X} renders as the text ${X}). Any other $ not followed by { is plain text — $9 and $(pwd) must survive.
- Options{Strict: true}: a well-formed ${VAR} reference to a key absent from the map is an error; lenient substitutes the empty string. Present-but-empty is never an error. Malformed references (unterminated ${, empty or bad name, junk before the }) are errors in BOTH modes.
- Errors carry a position: line:col, 1-based, column of the $ (for RenderFile, prefixed by the file shown relative to the root file's directory — a.conf:3:7 style — so an error inside an include names the included file). Say undefined variable "NAME" so people can grep.

Includes (RenderFile only; Expand passes the directive through as text):
- A line whose content is @include "relative/path" (leading/trailing whitespace ok, nothing else on the line) splices in the rendered content of that file. Anything else on the line — unquoted path, trailing junk — is a malformed @include error with the position of the @. An @include appearing mid-line is ordinary text.
- Paths resolve relative to the directory of the INCLUDING file, and the path itself gets variable substitution first (we do @include "${PROFILE}.conf").
- The directive line is replaced entirely (indentation included). If the included content doesn't end in a newline, add one so the next line isn't glued on. Empty file: contributes nothing.
- A file including itself, directly or through a chain, is an error naming the whole chain: include cycle: a.conf -> b.conf -> a.conf. Including the same file twice on parallel branches (diamond) is fine — it renders twice.
- Missing include file: error with the includer's position and the path as written.

Acceptance tests: render_test.go, protected. go test -race -timeout 30s ./... must pass.
